pāragāmino.meaning: going beyond; those who go to the far shore
pāragāmino.gram: adjective pāragāmī, nominative plural (or genitive singular)

pāramessanti.meaning: they will go beyond; they will reach the far shore
pāramessanti.gram: compound verb, 3rd person plural future (pāraṁ + eti)
breakdown: The niggahīta joins the following vowel as m.

pāramī.meaning: perfection; completeness; the qualities to be perfected by one who walks the path
breakdown: Highest, supreme (para “beyond” + superlative -ma); lengthened to pārama-.
Feminine abstract suffix: the state of supremacy, perfection.
pāramī.goenka: Pāramī or pāramitā (perfection, completeness) is a post-canonical expression describing the qualities of a person who walks the path. The necessary completeness refers to ten special qualities that need to be developed and perfected: sīla (morality), nekkhamma (renunciation), viriya (effort), khanti (tolerance), sacca (truthfulness), adhiṭṭhāna (strong determination), paññā (wisdom), upekhā (equanimity), mettā (selfless love) and dāna (generosity). Every sitting of meditation, every act of selfless service, develops these pāramīs and brings the meditator closer to the final goal.

pāramī or pāramitā.gram: feminine abstract noun (two equivalent forms of the same term)
pāramī or pāramitā.summary: Both pāramī and pāramitā denote the perfections developed by one walking the path; see the entry pāramī.
breakdown: The same term with the alternative abstract suffix -tā.

pāraṁ.meaning: beyond; across; the other side, the far shore

pāraṅgamasuttaṁ.meaning: the Pāraṅgama Sutta (“Discourse on Going Beyond”)
breakdown: Beyond, the far shore; the niggahīta becomes ṅ before g.

pārimaṁ.meaning: the farther; on the other side

pārimaṁ the.meaning: the farther; on the other side
pārimaṁ the.gram: glossary artifact; adjective pārima, accusative singular
pārimaṁ the.note: uncertain: the headword is a run-on glossary line for pārimaṁ “the farther (shore)”.

pārimaṁ tīraṁ.meaning: the farther shore; the other bank (of the river)
pārimaṁ tīraṁ.gram: phrase: adjective + neuter noun, accusative singular
pārimaṁ tīraṁ.goenka: “The farther shore” is the canonical image for nibbāna: in the Saṅgāravasuttaṁ (lesson 1.3.1) the practitioner crosses the flood of defilements from this shore — the world of craving — to the safety of the other bank by walking the path.

pāsarāsisuttaṁ.meaning: the Pāsarāsi Sutta (“Discourse on the Heap of Snares”, Opammavagga, Mūlapaṇṇāsapāḷi, Majjhimanikāya), also called the Ariyapariyesanā Sutta
pāsarāsisuttaṁ.goenka: This discourse records what the Buddha said after Brahma Sahampati requested him to teach the Dhamma to those who would be able to understand it (see the introduction to 1.3.1 Saṅgāravasuttaṁ — The Questions of Saṅgāravo).

pāsarāsisuttaṁ,.meaning: the Pāsarāsi Sutta, also named Ariyapariyesanā Sutta (Opammavagga, Mūlapaṇṇāsapāḷi, Majjhimanikāya)

breakdown: Adjectival suffix: that which inspires pasāda.

pāsāṇalekhūpamo.meaning: like a carving on rock; the simile of writing on stone
pāsāṇalekhūpamo.summary: From the simile of the three persons compared to writing on rock, on earth and on water — the rock-carving stands for anger (and saṅkhāra) of the deepest, most lasting kind.
breakdown: Simile, comparison; lekha + upama coalesces to lekhūpama.

pāto/pātar.gram: indeclinable adverb (two forms of the same word)

pātubhāvasuttaṁ.meaning: the Pātubhāva Sutta (“Discourse on Appearance, Manifestation”)

pātubhāvo.gram: masculine abstract noun (from pātubhavati), nominative singular
breakdown: Becoming, state: “coming into the open”, manifestation.

pātukaroti.gram: compound verb, 3rd person singular present (pātu + karoti)

pāvisi.gram: verb, aorist 3rd person singular of pavisati (pa + √vis)
breakdown: Prefix “into” (with augment a, lengthened to pā-).

pāṇa.meaning: breathing being; living being; breath, life
breakdown: Root meaning “to breathe”: that which breathes, a living being.
pāṇa.note: uncertain: the lexicon's gloss line carries the larger compound sabbapāṇabhūtahitānukampī “out of compassion for the benefit of all breathing beings”; only “breathing being” belongs to pāṇa.

pāṇabhūtatthi.meaning: there are living beings (run-together phrase: pāṇabhūtā + atthi)
breakdown: There is, there are (the vowels fuse at the boundary).

pāṇaghāto.meaning: the slaughter of living beings; destruction of life

pāṇampi.meaning: even a living being; and a living being
pāṇampi.gram: masculine noun pāṇa, accusative singular + emphatic particle pi
breakdown: Even, and; the niggahīta becomes m before p.

pāṇasaññino.meaning: conscious of (it being) a living being; aware that it is a being
pāṇasaññino.gram: adjective pāṇasaññī, nominative plural (or genitive singular)

pāṇissaraṁ.meaning: hand-clapping; music made by clapping the hands
breakdown: Sound; sandhi doubling of s at the boundary.

pāṇisu.note: uncertain: the form is the locative plural of pāṇi (“hand”) but the lexicon glosses it “in beings” (as if from pāṇa); follow the lexicon for the sutta context.

pāṇupetaṁ.meaning: endowed with life; as long as life lasts (in the refuge formula: “for life”)
breakdown: Endowed with, possessed of (pāṇa + upeta coalesce to pāṇupeta).

pāṇātipāto.meaning: killing living beings; destruction of life
breakdown: Striking down, slaying (ati + √pat); the vowels coalesce to pāṇāti-.
pāṇātipāto.goenka: Pāṇātipāto, the killing of living beings, is the first of the unwholesome actions abandoned in sīla: the first precept is abstaining from killing any being. Sīla is the indispensable base of the path — without abstaining from harming beings, the mind cannot become concentrated or develop liberating wisdom.

pāṇātipātā.meaning: from killing living beings (abstention from the destruction of life)
breakdown: That which breathes, a living being.
pāṇātipātā.goenka: Pāṇātipātā veramaṇī — abstaining from killing — is the first precept. The lesson introducing it cites Dhammo have rakkhati dhammacāriṁ, “truly, Dhamma protects those who foster the Dhamma”: a universal truth of moral reciprocity akin to “you reap what you sow”. One who lives by the Dhamma finds protection through the complete abandonment of the unwholesome and the cultivation of the wholesome, and the beneficial results experienced in daily life reinforce the dhammacāri's resolve.

pāṭihāriyapakkhañca.meaning: and the special (extra) observance period: keeping the Uposatha continuously for three months, or at least one month, during the rainy season
pāṭihāriyapakkhañca.gram: masculine noun, compound, accusative singular + conjunction ca
breakdown: Fortnight, period (of the moon); the niggahīta becomes ñ before c.

pāṭikaṅkhaṁ.gram: future passive participle of paṭikaṅkhati, neuter/accusative singular
breakdown: Participial ending: “what is to be expected”.

pāṭipado.meaning: following the path in the correct manner; one practising the way
breakdown: Root meaning “to step, to go”: relating to paṭipadā, the way of practice.

pūjanīyānaṁ.meaning: of those who should be honoured; of those worthy of veneration
pūjanīyānaṁ.gram: future passive participle of pūjeti, genitive/dative plural

pūtikāyena.meaning: with this putrid body; by the rotten body

breakdown: Root meaning “to be apart, to be alone”.

rajata.meaning: silver (in the compound jātarūparajatapaṭiggahaṇā: acceptance of gold and silver)
breakdown: Root meaning “to shine, to be coloured”.
rajata.note: uncertain: the lexicon's gloss line carries the larger compound (jātarūpa)rajatapaṭiggahaṇā “gold + silver + acceptance”; only “silver” belongs to rajata.

rajjuyā.meaning: with a rope; by a string

rajopatho.meaning: a path of dust; the dusty (impure) way (said of household life)

rakkhatha.gram: verb, 2nd person plural imperative of rakkhati (√rakkh)

rakkhāvaraṇaguttiṁ.meaning: protection, shelter and guarding (a set phrase for complete protection)

rassakā.gram: adjective rassaka (rassa + ka), nominative plural or feminine singular

ratanacaṅkamacetiya.meaning: the Jewel Walk Shrine (Ratanacaṅkama-cetiya, marking the Buddha's walking meditation after the awakening)
breakdown: Walking up and down, walking meditation.

ratanasutta.meaning: the Ratana Sutta (“Discourse on the Jewels”)
breakdown: Gem, jewel — the three jewels of Buddha, Dhamma and Saṅgha.
ratanasutta.goenka: The Ratanasutta is the sutta recited on day two of the 10-day meditation course by S. N. Goenka, as part of the selection of suttas chanted (along with Hindi verses) through the course.
